label: Yirmeyahu (Jeremiah) — Part 7 — Chapters 49–52
summary_plain: YHWH pronounces judgment on the nations: Ammon, Edom, Damascus, Qedar, Chatsor, Elam, and above all — Babylon. Two full chapters (50-51) declare Babylon's destruction: She who sits on many waters, rich in treasures — her end has come. Babylon was a golden cup in YHWH's hand, making all the earth drunk. YHWH says: I am against you, O destroying mountain — hammer of the whole earth. Flee from the midst of Babylon — let every one save his life. Chapter 52 recounts the fall of Yerushalayim — the siege, the breach, the burning of the Temple, the exile — confirming everything Yirmeyahu prophesied.
summary_scholarly: The ACR reads from the DSS Jeremiah manuscripts (4QJer-a through 4QJer-e) and the Orit Ge'ez. Chapters 50-51 constitute the longest single oracle against a foreign power in the prophetic literature — two chapters devoted to Babylon's judgment. The imagery is dense and multi-layered: Babylon as golden cup (51:7), as dwelling on many waters (51:13), as hammer of the whole earth (50:23), as destroying mountain (51:25). These images are reused in Revelation 17-18 almost verbatim — the whore of Babylon sitting on many waters, the golden cup, the call to flee. Chapter 52 is nearly identical to 2 Melakhim 25 — a historical appendix confirming the prophetic word. The DSS fragments and the LXX confirm significant textual variants in the Babylon oracles.

key_terms.definition: Yirmeyahu 51:7: Babylon was a golden cup in YHWH's hand, making all the earth drunk. All the nations drank of her wine — therefore the nations went mad. The image describes Babylon as YHWH's instrument of judgment that itself becomes subject to judgment.

Yirmeyahu 51:13: 'O you who dwell on many waters, rich in treasures — your end has come.' Babylon sat on the Euphrates river system. The image is reused in Revelation 17:1 for the eschatological Babylon — 'the great harlot who sits on many waters.'

key_terms.term: Hammer of the Whole Earth (Pattish Kol Ha'Aretz)
key_terms.definition: Yirmeyahu 50:23: 'How the hammer of the whole earth is cut down and broken!' Babylon is YHWH's hammer — the instrument used to shatter nations. But the hammer itself will be shattered.

Brother of Barukh, the quartermaster. Yirmeyahu gives him a scroll containing all the words against Babylon. When he arrives in Babylon, he is to read it aloud and then tie a stone to the scroll and cast it into the Euphrates, saying: Thus shall Babylon sink (51:59-64).

faq.question: What are the parallels between Yirmeyahu 50-51's 'dwelling on many waters,' 'hammer of the whole earth,' and 'golden cup' — and how are they reused?
faq.answer: Yirmeyahu 50-51 builds a composite portrait of Babylon through three defining images. First: the golden cup (51:7) — Babylon was a golden cup in YHWH's hand, making all the earth drunk with her wine. Second: dwelling on many waters (51:13) — she sits on the Euphrates river system, rich in treasures, but her end has come. Third: the hammer of the whole earth (50:23) — Babylon is YHWH's instrument of destruction against nations, but the hammer itself is cut down and broken. These three images reappear almost verbatim in Revelation 17-18: the great harlot 'sits on many waters' (17:1), holds a 'golden cup full of abominations' (17:4), and the nations 'have drunk of the wine of the wrath of her fornication' (14:8). The Revelation text is not creating new imagery — it is directly quoting Yirmeyahu's Babylon oracle and applying it to an eschatological successor. Every empire that assumes the role of Babylon — dominating nations, controlling economies, intoxicating peoples with its culture — inherits the same judgment. The prophetic text provides the permanent template: whatever power sits on many waters, holds the golden cup, and hammers the whole earth will be cut down. Yirmeyahu established the pattern. Revelation reapplied it. The covenant reader applies it to whatever system currently occupies that structural position.

faq.question: Why does Yirmeyahu end with a historical appendix (chapter 52) repeating the fall of Yerushalayim?
faq.answer: Chapter 52 is nearly identical to 2 Melakhim 25. It recounts the siege, the breach of the wall, the capture of Tsidqiyahu, the burning of the Temple, the exile of the population, and the count of those deported. Its placement at the end of Yirmeyahu serves a specific function: prophetic vindication. Everything Yirmeyahu prophesied — the destruction the officials mocked him for predicting, the exile they imprisoned him for announcing, the fall they beat him for declaring — happened exactly as he said. The historical appendix is YHWH's signature: the word spoken through the prophet was fulfilled. For the covenant people in every generation: when the institutional leadership rejects the prophetic word, the prophetic word still comes to pass. The officials who threw Yirmeyahu in the cistern perished. The word Yirmeyahu spoke endured.

faq.question: How does the sinking of the scroll in the Euphrates (51:59-64) function as a prophetic sign?
faq.answer: Yirmeyahu gives Serayah a scroll containing all the words against Babylon and instructs him: when you arrive, read it aloud, then tie a stone to it and cast it into the Euphrates, saying — thus shall Babylon sink to rise no more. The physical act — the stone, the water, the sinking — is the prophetic sign that enacts the judgment. The word is not merely spoken — it is performed. The same pattern operates throughout the prophetic literature: Yehezkel lies on his side, Yirmeyahu wears a yoke, Hoshea marries. The prophetic word is embodied. The scroll sinks into the river that sustains Babylon — the many waters on which she dwells become the waters of her burial. The Great Eraser removed prophetic action from worship — replacing embodied covenant practice with passive pew-sitting. The prophetic tradition is active, physical, and performative.
